coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble, lignin-derived carbon dots with high fluorescent emissions and their applications in bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Myint, Aye Aye
Rhim, Won-Kyu
Nam, Jwa-Min
Kim, Jaehoon
Lee, Youn-Woo
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of water-soluble and environmentally friendly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using the renewable resource lignin as a precursor, which is a waste emitted from pulp and paper industries and lignocellulosic biofuel production, using a compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent method followed by carbonization and chemical oxidation with an acid mixture (H2SO4:HNO3 =1:3, v/v) at 90°C for 8, 16, and 24h. The as-synthesized CDs emitted bright yellow fluorescence (FL) under argon laser excitation at 458nm with excitation-wavelength-independent and excitation-wavelength-dependent FL emission characteristics. Moreover, the CDs exhibited excellent stability in a highly concentrated NaCl solution, photostability with pH-dependent FL emission activities, and cellular imaging capability with very low cytotoxicity. Notably, the FL emission intensities of the as-synthesized CDs increased with longer reaction times, indicating high degree of oxidation on the CD surface and consequently, high quantum yield of 13% was achieved. Therefore, the as-synthesized CDs can be used as promising fluorescent probes for bioimaging applications in vitro and in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon dots
Tunable fluorescence properties
High photostability
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent CDs from lignin
Physicochemical and surface properties of fluorescent CDs
Optical and FL properties of fluorescent CDs

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic of synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using kraft lignin as a precursor, and photographs of the raw material and products at each stage (clCO2 =compressed liquid CO2). (B–D) Photographs of CDs-24. B (a and b): The color intensity is dependent on the concentration of the CDs-24 solution in DI water; C (a–c): the fluorescent colors emitted under daylight, UV light at 365nm, and argon laser excitation at 458nm observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope for the CDs-24 solution in DI water; D (a and b): solubility pattern of the as-synthesized CDs-24.

(A) TEM image of the as-synthesized CDs-24 (a), and the inset figure shows the particle size distribution measured from the TEM image; HR-TEM image of CDs-24 (b); corresponding 2D FFT image (c). (B) AFM topography images of CDs-24 in ethanol deposited on a silicon wafer substrate: 2D(a) and 3D(b); and height profile of along the line on the 2D image (c). (C) SEM/EDS elemental mapping images of CDs-24 in ethanol deposited on a silicon wafer substrate (magnification: 1000×).

(A) XRD patterns, (B) Raman spectra, and (C) FTIR spectra of CNPs, CDs-8, CDs-16, and CDs-24. SOH: OH stretching of phenolic and aliphatic moieties, S
COOH: stretching of carboxylic acid, SCO: CO stretching, SCC: CC stretching, δipOH: OH in-plane deformation of phenolic OH, δ CO CO deformation in secondary alcohols and aliphatic ether, and δopArCH: aromatic CH out-of-plane deformation of the guaiacyl unit.
(A) XPS survey spectra of CNPs, CDs-8, CDs-16, and CDs-24, and core-level XPS (B) C 1s and (C) O 1s spectra of CDs-24.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL emission spectra of the fluorescent CDs-24 in DI water at excitation wavelengths of 280–640nm with a progressive increment of 20nm and the corresponding 3D mesh plot of maximum FL emission intensity and wavelength at the excitation wavelengths of 280–640nm (inset). (B) Comparison of the UV–vis absorption spectra and maximum FL emission intensities at an excitation wavelength of 500nm for CDs-8 (orange), CDs-16 (yellow green), and CDs-24 (green) in DI water. The concentration of all the samples was fixed at 0.025mgmL−1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Normalized FL emission intensities (recorded at an excitation of 440nm) of CDs-8, CDs-16, and CDs-24 with respect to pH from 2.0 to 12.5. (B) Their fluorescent (recorded at an excitation of 440nm) stability at various NaCl salt concentrations, and C) under UV light at 365nm (UV365) for 0–24h; photographs of CDs-24 under UV365 for 0–24h (inset) (concentration of CDs-24 solution=1mgmL−1 in DI water). (D) Time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decay curve of CDs-24 recorded at room temperature and an emission wavelength of 530nm; respective lifetime data and parameters generated by the exponential fitting (τ avg =average lifetime decay components) (inset).

Relative cell viability of HeLa cells (normalized to the untreated control) after incubation with CDs-8, CDs-16, and CDs-24 at various concentrations for 48h. The error bars are the standard deviations of triplicate samples.

(A) CLSM bright field images of HeLa cells, and (B) individual nuclei stained with Hoechst dye (blue). (C) CLSM fluorescent image of HeLa cells labeled with CDs-24 (green), and (D) overlay image of nuclei stained with Hoechst dye (blue) and CDs-24 (green) for 4h. The scale bar is 20μm. The concentration of CDs-24 is 100μgmL−1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of optical characteristics and applications of the CDs derived from different carbon precursors with the reported methods.

row.entry: Human hepatocellular liver carcinoma (HepG2) cells imaging
In vivo FL imaging in mouse
M. tuberculosis, P. aeruginosa and M. oryzae imaging
In vivo imaging of orthotopic C6 glioma-bearing mice
Selective Fe3+ ion sensing and fungal imaging
Selective Fe3+ ion sensing, fluorescence ink and cellular imaging (yeast and Cone 9 hapatocytes rat liver cells)
Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) and HeLa cells imaging
MDA-MB-231 cells imaging and oxygen reduction reaction
clCO2 antisolvent method followed by carbonization and oxidation with an acid mixture (3H2SO4:HNO3)

head.ce:title: Water-soluble, lignin-derived carbon dots with high fluorescent emissions and their applications in bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering, College of Engineering and Institute of Chemical Processes (ICP), Seoul National University, Seoul 151–744, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering
College of Engineering and Institute of Chemical Processes (ICP)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, and SKKU Advanced Institute of Nano Technology, Sungkyunkwan University, 2066, Seobu-Ro, Jangan-Gu, Suwon, Gyeong Gi-Do 16419, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, and SKKU Advanced Institute of Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Seoul National University, Seoul 151–747, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of water-soluble and environmentally friendly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using the renewable resource lignin as a precursor, which is a waste emitted from pulp and paper industries and lignocellulosic biofuel production, using a compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent method followed by carbonization and chemical oxidation with an acid mixture (H2SO4:HNO3 =1:3, v/v) at 90°C for 8, 16, and 24h. The as-synthesized CDs emitted bright yellow fluorescence (FL) under argon laser excitation at 458nm with excitation-wavelength-independent and excitation-wavelength-dependent FL emission characteristics. Moreover, the CDs exhibited excellent stability in a highly concentrated NaCl solution, photostability with pH-dependent FL emission activities, and cellular imaging capability with very low cytotoxicity. Notably, the FL emission intensities of the as-synthesized CDs increased with longer reaction times, indicating high degree of oxidation on the CD surface and consequently, high quantum yield of 13% was achieved. Therefore, the as-synthesized CDs can be used as promising fluorescent probes for bioimaging applications in vitro and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in diverse applications, including bioimaging [1–9], drug delivery [5–9], sensing [9–11], photocatalysis [12–14], and electronic nanodevices [15] owing to their fascinating and favorable properties such as water solubility, chemical stability, environmental friendliness, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photostability, non-blinking photoluminescence, tunable photoluminescence, versatile surface functionality, and easy accessibility as compared to traditional fluorescent organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and other carbon nanomaterials (e.g. graphene and carbon nanotubes) [12]. Typically, CDs are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with sizes below 10nm and are collectively defined as a carbogenic core with surface functional groups [16–18]. CDs are comprised of an amorphous to nanocrystalline core with predominantly graphitic or turbostatic sp2 -hybridized carbon whose surface is modified with oxygenic moieties [19–22]. In fact, the physico-chemical and optical properties of CDs are governed by their high aspect ratio, surface defects, and surface functionalities, which also strongly depend on the carbon precursors and synthetic routes [23,24].

Over the past decade, various synthesis routes have been developed to synthesize CDs and control their morphology and/or size; these synthesis routes can generally be classified into two kinds of approaches: “top-down” and “bottom-up” [13,23]. Although the previous approaches could produce high-quality CDs, they have some limitations. For example, the precursors for CDs were based on petroleum-based products and the synthetic routes typically involved complicated, multi-step operations and used a large amount of organic solvents, which produced chemical wastes as byproducts. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop an environmentally friendly and economically sustainable route for practical-scale production of CDs. Furthermore, with the recent advances in materials science and nanobioengineering, carbon nanomaterials from natural resources have become highly attractive for biomedical applications because of their biodegradability, biocompatibility, and large-scale availability at a low cost [18]. Importantly, high-quantum-yield CDs is a crucial in its efficient utilization of fluorescent bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery and therapy [25] and the development of high fluorescence (FL) intensity of CDs is still challenging [1]. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop a green, sustainable, cost-effective, and scalable method to synthesize CDs from renewable carbon precursors with suitable functional groups.

In recent years, many efforts have been devoted to develop facile and green chemistry-based methods for the preparation of fluorescent CDs using various ecofriendly materials of natural products and waste derived from biomass as the carbon source; this includes apple juice [2], Saccharum officinarum (sugar cane) juice [3], Solanum tuberosum (potato) [4], dried shrimp [5], Phyllantus acidus [9], Chionanthus retusus fruit extract [11], unripe peach [26], unripe Prunus mume fruit extract [27], waste frying oil [28], food wastes [29], and dairy waste [30]. As the largest natural aromatic biopolymer on earth and a major component of lignocellulosic biomass, lignin is an ideal candidate for preparing bulk aromatic chemicals and value-added carbon materials because of its high carbon content with unique aromatic structure and chemical composition [31–33]. However, there are some difficulties associated with lignin utilization as carbon-based nanomaterials due to its intrinsic nature of complex aromatic structure and different properties based on its source of origin or type of separation/production methods [34,35]. To date, only three studies have been documented for producing fluorescent CDs from lignin. Chen et al. [36] reported as the first study for the synthesis of CDs using lignin through a hydrothermal treatment with H2O2 as an oxidizing agent. However, they did not describe the source or type of lignin and quantum yield of the resulted CDs, which could not fully discuss the advantages of lignin as the carbon source of fluorescent CDs. Very recently, Niu et al. [37] reported a simple and green molecular aggregation method for preparing CDs (size: ∼2.4nm) with a quantum yield of 1.68% in water and 2.47% in the solid state from cellulolytic enzyme lignin and used as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging. Similarly, Rai et al. [38] reported the synthesis of water-soluble reduced fluorescent CDs (size: ∼4.9nm) with a quantum yield of 47.3% using sulfur containing lignosulfonate lignin as a carbon source. Sulfur acted as a doping agent through microwave irradiation after reduction with NaBH4. The reduced fluorescent CDs used as a fluorescent probe for nano-drug-carrier and cellular imaging. Despite the inspiration of the achievements, they did not report yields of CDs products which is an important in a large-scale production. Here, we report the first study of a facile, cost-efficient and scalable method for the production of water-soluble fluorescent CDs (∼2.4nm) with bright yellow FL emission under argon excitation at 458nm and tunable FL characteristics and excellent chemical- and photo-stability using commercial kraft lignin as a precursor. The chemical structure and functional groups present in kraft lignin are different with the types of lignins used in the previous reports. For instance, kraft lignin includes hydrophilic aliphatic thiol groups with a low sulfur content (1–2wt%) as compared to lignosulfonate lignin (sulfur content: 3.5–8.0wt%). Even though cellulolytic enzyme lignin contains basic subunits and linkages that are similar to those of kraft or lignosulfonate lignin, but the chemical structure of cellulolytic enzyme lignin is very similar to sulphur-free native lignin [39,40]. Previously, we demonstrated that kraft lignin can be utilized to produce lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) [39] using compressed liquid carbon dioxide (clCO2) as an antisolvent and hierarchically porous nanocarbon [41] via thermostabilization and carbonization of the LNPs. Herein, to explore the potential biomedical applications of lignin-derived carbons, we synthesized highly water-soluble and fluorescent CDs with high quantum yield (QY) directly from lignin. The synthetic approach for producing CDs from lignin consisted of two steps: first, CNPs were synthesized using the clCO2 antisolvent method followed by thermostabilization and carbonization. Subsequently, the as-synthesized CNPs were oxidized to produce water-soluble and fluorescent CDs. The oxidation conditions were adjusted to control the physicochemical, surface state, optical, and photoluminescent (PL) properties of the synthesized CDs. Thus, we attempted to gain an understanding of the origin of PL emission behavior in the CDs. Owing to potential applications in bio-imaging and related therapies, cytotoxicity tests and cell imaging of the synthesized CDs were conducted using HeLa cells (breast cancer cell lines), which are commonly used as model cells for bioimaging.

Ethanol (99.9%), NaOH (98.0%) in bead form, HCl (35–37%), and HNO3 (60%) were purchased from Samchun Pure Chemical Co. (South Korea), and H2SO4 (98%) was purchased from PFP (Japan). All the chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. Kraft (Alkali) lignin was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) and used as received. Rodamine B (for fluorescence), coumarin 153 (dye content 99%), phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH: 7.4, BioReagents), NaCl (≥99.5%), and dialysis tubing (high-retention seamless cellulose tubing, 99.99% retention) with a molecular-weight cut-off (MWCO) of 12.4kDa were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Cell culture media (Gibco™), such as Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), penicillin, streptomycin, and fetal bovine serum (FBS), and a blue fluorescence dye Hoechst 33342 (Invitrogen™) were supplied by Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Deionized water (DI) was prepared using a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent CDs from lignin
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs were synthesized from lignin via a two-step approach. The synthetic scheme of fluorescent CDs is shown in Fig. 1 A. First, CNPs were synthesized using commercial kraft lignin by first preparing LNPs via a clCO2 antisolvent method followed by subjecting the as-synthesized LNPs to thermostabilization and carbonization processes [32,33]. The schematic diagram of clCO2 antisolvent apparatus and detailed description of the experimental procedures for synthesizing LNPs and CNPs from kraft lignin are provided in the supplementary data (Fig. S1). The acid oxidation of the as-synthesized CNPs was carried out using a slight modification of the method documented in previous reports on the synthesis of carbon nanostructures, CDs, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from various carbon sources such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, and carbon fibers [42–45]. In brief, 100mg of CNPs was added to a 20mL acid mixture (H2SO4:HNO3 =3:1, v/v) in a 250mL borosilicate bottle, and the solution was sonicated for 1h. The mixture of CNPs and acids was thereafter heated to 90°C in an oil bath under vigorous stirring for 8, 16, and 24h. The oxidation time was controlled to elucidate the effects of reaction time on the physicochemical properties of the CDs. After cooling down to 25±2°C, the reaction mixture was dispersed in 100mL of DI water and filtered through a G4 Pyrex glass filter (IWAKI, Japan) to remove unreacted carbon residues. The filtrate was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 12.4kDa) against DI water for 2d until the pH value of DI water was ∼7.0 to remove the excess acid. Subsequently, the water in the dialyzed CD solution was evaporated under vacuum, and the obtained wet powder was dried in a vacuum oven to achieve a dry powder of CDs. The percentages of the overall recovery yields of CDs were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2).

For raw kraft lignin as a starting material,
(1)
Y CDsfromKL (%)=(m CDs/m 0 KL)×100

For the as-synthesized CNPs as a starting material,
(2)
Y CDsfromCNPs (%)=(m CDs/m 0 CNPs)×100
where Y CDsfromKL and Y CDsfromCNPs are the percentage yields of the CDs from raw kraft lignin and the as-synthesized CNPs, respectively; m CDs is the mass (in grams) of the synthesized CDs; m 0 KL and m 0 CNPs are the respective masses (in grams) of raw kraft lignin and the CNPs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were acquired using JEM 3010 HR-TEM (JEOL, Japan) operated at an acceleration voltage of 300kV with a GATAN Ultrascan CCD camera after drop-casting the CDs suspended in ethanol onto super-thin carbon film coated 300-mesh copper grids (Electron Microscopy Sciences, USA) and air drying the grids overnight. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired using a NanostationII AFM/SPM operating in the tapping mode, and the height of the samples was analyzed by using the Scanning Probe Image Processor (SPIP) software. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) element mapping images were acquired using a SURRA 55VP FE-SEM (Carl Zeiss, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Rigaku Miniflex X-ray diffractometer (Japan) equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ =0.15406nm) operated at 40kV and 200mA. Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM HR800 micro-Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA) equipped with an Alpha300R confocal Raman microscope (WITec, USA) using a 100× objective lens at room temperature and incident laser light of wavelength 532nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained via the KBr pellet method using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) with a resolution of 4cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out by using an AXIS-His XPS instrument (Kratos Analytical Ltd., Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with an Mg Kα X-ray source operated at 150W and a charge neutralizer. XPS spectra were recorded using an analyzer pass energy of 20eV and step size of 0.1eV per step. The quantification was performed using the default relative sensitivity factor values supplied by the XPS manufacturer. Data analysis was performed using CasaXPS software. Two-point energy stable referencing was performed using adventitious C (284.5eV) and valence bond energy corrections. The percentages of individual elements detected were determined from the relative composition analysis of the peak areas of the bands. Zeta potential measurements were performed using an ELSZ-1000 (Photal Otsuka Electronics Co. Ltd., Japan). Silicon wafer substrates were used for AFM, SEM/EDS, Raman, and XPS analyses of the samples. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded at 25°C using an Evolution 201 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific Inc., USA). Fluorescence (FL) measurements were performed using a FP8300 Fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco Instruments Inc., Australia). The photostability of CDs was tested using an ENF 240C UV lamp (Spectroline, USA). Time-resolved photoluminescence decay measurements were conducted via time-correlated single-photon counting technique using a FluoTime 200 modular fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (PicoQuant, Germany). Picosecond diode lasers were used as the excitation source, and decay curves were acquired by fitting the analysis data using PicoQuant FluoFit software program. Rhodamine B was used as an indicator dye to monitor the interfacial charge-transfer, and the FL intensities of the samples were measured at an emission wavelength of 530nm. The QY of the CDs was obtained by comparing the integrated FL intensities (excited at 440nm) and absorbance values (at 440nm) of the CD solution with those of the reference coumarin 153 in ethanol solution (QY=0.58).

HeLa cells, used as model cells, were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, USA). The viability of the cells was evaluated using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8, Dojindo Lab., Japan) followed by 2-(2-methoxy-4-nitrophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium monosodium salt (WST-8) colorimetric method [47] after treatment with the synthesized CDs. HeLa cells (approximately 1.6×104 cells per well) were seeded in a 96-well microplate containing 100μgmL−1 streptomycin and 10% (v/v) FBS at 37°C in 5% CO2. After seeding for 24h, various concentrations of three types of CDs in cultured media (from 16μgmL−1 to 500μgmL−1) were added into each well in triplicate, and the cells were allowed to grow for further 48h. The cells not subjected to treatment with CDs were considered as a control. Subsequently, 10μL of the CCK-8 solution was added into each well followed by incubation for 2h at 37°C in 5% CO2. The optical density (OD) of yellow-colored formazan dye, generated by the activities of dehydrogenases in cells, was measured at the wavelength of 450nm using a Synergy Mx multi-mode microplate reader (BioTek Instruments Inc., USA). The intensity of OD indicates the number of living cells, and the percentages of viable cells relative to the untreated control were plotted against CDs concentrations.

HeLa cells (approximately 3×104 cells per well) were plated in a 35-mm-thick glass bottom culture dish (MatTek, USA) containing 200μL of DMEM supplemented with 100UmL−1 penicillin, 100μgmL−1 streptomycin, and 10% (v/v) FBS, and seeded at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After seeding for 24h, various concentration of CDs in a culture medium (50, 100 and 200μgmL−1) was added to the dish followed by incubation for 4h to confirm the applicability of CDs to cellular imaging. Thereafter, the HeLa cells treated with CDs were gently washed three times with 1×PBS solution to remove excess CDs. For labeling the cell nucleus, 2μgmL−1 Hoechst dye was mixed with HeLa cells by gentle shaking followed by incubation for 20min and subsequently washing with 1× PBS solution three times. Cell images were visualized using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM 710, Carl Zeiss, Germany) with a 40× objective, and analyzed using the Zen imaging software (Germany).

Fig. 1A illustrates the process of producing fluorescent CDs using kraft lignin as a precursor. Hereafter, the various synthesized CDs are denoted as CDs-8, CDs-16, and CDs-24 according to their respective reaction times 8, 16, and 24h. The as-synthesized CDs-24 exhibited brown-to-yellow colors depending on the concentration in DI water (Fig. 1B(a and b)). The light yellow transparent liquid (under daylight) emitted pale yellow FL under UV light at 365nm (UV365), whereas bright yellow FL under argon laser excitation at 458nm was observed using CLSM (Fig. 1C(a–c)). The CDs-24 was highly soluble in water and well dispersed in ethanol (Fig. 1D(a and b)). These properties are similar to those of CDs and GQDs synthesized from various starting materials such as paper ash, pitch carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, graphite, petroleum coke, glucose, and dextrin [43,44,47–50], indicating that highly fluorescent QDs can be produced from kraft lignin. In addition, the zeta potential values of CDs-24 in ethanol and PBS (pH=7.4) were measured to be −17.66±0.35mV and −31.08±1.89mV, respectively, confirming the high degree of dispersion stability of the synthesized CDs [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical and surface properties of fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents typical surface morphologies of the as-synthesized CDs-24 derived from kraft lignin. A representative TEM image and particle size distribution data are shown in Fig. 2A(a) and inset figure illustrating that CDs-24 consists of well-dispersed, quasi-spherical, and nanometer-sized particles with an average diameter of ∼2.4nm and very narrow size distribution. As shown in the HR-TEM and the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) images in Fig. 2A(b and c), CDs-24 has a nanocrystalline structure consisting of parallel crystal planes with a lattice spacing of ∼0.29nm, which is higher than the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene (∼0.24nm) [43,51,52]. The enlarged nanocrystalline structure can be attributed to the influence of lattice defects in the carbon nanostructures of the CNPs caused by chemical oxidation and exfoliation. The AFM image of CDs-24 shown in Fig. 2B(a–c) reveal that CDs-24 has a topographic height in the range 0.23–0.65nm, indicating that most of the CDs consist of 1–2 graphene layers [43,47,51,53–56]. SEM/EDS elemental mapping images in Fig. 2C(a–c) confirm that CDs-24 deposited on a silicon wafer substrate forms a thin film with evenly distributed C and O atoms on its surface. As shown in Fig. S2, CDs-8 and CDs-16 exhibit similar lattice planes and sizes as CDs-24 with the lattice spacing of ∼0.283nm and ∼0.288nm, respectively, and average diameter of ∼2.42±0.41nm and 2.43±0.35nm, respectively. The AFM height profile data of CDs-8 and CDs-16 (Fig. S3) indicate that most of these CDs contain 1–2 graphene layers.

Fig. 3 Ashows the typical XRD profiles of the amorphous CNPs and as-synthesized CDs. The CDs show a broad, amorphous (002) peak centered at 22.6° whose intensity is lower than that of CNPs, indicating that the CDs are smaller than the CNPs [39]. Notably, the interlayer spacing of the CDs is ∼3.9Å, which is larger than the spacing between the (002) planes in CNPs (∼3.7Å) and graphite (3.44Å) [47]. This enlarged spacing can be attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups introduced during the oxidation of CNPs, thereby enhancing the interlayer distance of the CDs. The Raman spectra of the CDs (Fig. 3B) exhibit an intense disorder (D) band at ∼1373cm−1 (which is related to the presence of sp3 defects) and a crystalline (G) band at ∼1593cm−1 (which is related to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbons). The relative intensity ratio of the D and G bands (I D/I G) of the CDs is smaller than that of the CNPs owing to the presence of oxygen-rich edges in the CDs. As the reaction time increased from 8 to 24h, the ID /IG ratio decreased from 0.943 to 0.927, suggesting that more amount of oxygen functionalities attached at the edge sites cause a higher degree of disorder in the graphene structure. The ID /IG value of CDs-24 is similar to that of GQDs [43,51]. The FTIR results illustrated in Fig. 3C further demonstrated that significant amounts of hydrophilic groups—indicated by alcoholic OH stretching (∼3431–3441cm−1), carboxylic acid OH stretching (∼2558cm−1), OH bending (∼1404–1441cm−1), CO stretching (∼1725cm−1), and CO stretching (∼1230cm−1) bands—are present on the surface of CDs. The enriched oxygen functionalities in CDs may be responsible for their good solubility and dispersibility in water and hydrophilic organic solvents.

To further investigate the chemical functionalization of CDs, XPS was employed and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The survey spectra indicate the presence of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p 2 in the CNPs and CDs (Fig. 4A). The elemental composition results calculated from their XPS spectra indicate that the oxygen contents in the CDs linearly increase with the increase in reaction time from 8h to 16h, but do not change any further after chemical oxidation for 24h (Table S1). The CDs are mainly composed of C (54.6–57.2wt%) and O (37.4–41.1wt%) with trace amounts of N (1.0–1.8wt%) and S (2.5–3.7wt%). As demonstrated in Figs. 4 and S4 and Table S2, CDs-24 has the highest number of OH, CO, COOH, or COC groups and the highest ratio of sp2 to sp3 carbon bonds (sp2 /sp3 ) as compared with CDs-8 and CDs-16, which is consistent with the FTIR and Raman results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and FL properties of fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 5A, S5 and S6, the CDs exhibit broad absorptions from 200 to 800nm with a maximum sharp peak at 263nm, which is ascribed to n–π* transitions of CO bonds. The FL emission spectra of CDs also contain wavelength-dependent excitation (when excited from 280nm to 460nm) and wavelength-independent excitation (when excited from 480nm to 640nm) characteristics, which reflect the effects of distribution of different emissive defect sites on the surface of each CD through chemical oxidation and the different-sized aromatic domains in the CDs [57]. The FL emission of CDs ranges the entire visible wavelength region of the spectra and extends to the near-infrared region, which accompanies with a decrease of the FL intensity when the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 640nm. The maximal FL emission intensities of the CDs are around 560nm when excited at 500nm. In fact, the trends for the absorption and maximal FL emission intensities are stronger with longer reaction times, as depicted in Fig. 5B. The FL QY of CDs-24 is 13.0%, whereas those of CDs-8 and CDs-16 are 8.1% and 11.6%, respectively; these values are much higher than the reported values of other CDs and GQDs (∼2–9%) and comparable with the reported values of nitrogen-doped CDs prepared from ecofriendly carbon sources (14–16%), as listed in Table 1 . The higher QY of the CDs synthesized in this study is attributed to the higher degree of oxidation on their surfaces, which is also evident from the FTIR, XPS, and elemental composition results.

As shown in Figs. 6A and S7, the CDs show pH-dependent FL activities. When pH increases from 2 to 6, the FL intensity of the CDs increases, but a further increase in pH to 13 results in a rapid loss in the FL intensity of the CDs. Similar trends have been reported for other types of CDs produced from urea and p-phenylenediamine [60], threonine [61], and chloroform and diethylamine [62]. The environmentally sensitive behaviors of the CDs are attributed to changes in the surface charge induced by protonation–deprotonation, which is beneficial for applications to molecular sensing probes [13,63]. The stability in NaCl solution and photostability of the CDs synthesized from kraft lignin were tested. The CDs exhibit excellent stability in NaCl solutions at concentrations up to 800mM (Figs. 6B and S8) and excellent photostability under UV365 for 24h (Figs. 6C and S9). Notably, the FL emission intensity of CD solutions in DI water (concentration: 0.1mgmL−1) gradually increases with UV365 exposure time up to 14.5%, 22.2%, and 13.4% for CDs-8, CDs-16, and CDs-24, respectively. The increase in the FL intensity of the CD solutions might be due to the increase in the evaporation of water or the increase in the optical density of the CDs molecules with the increase in the UV365 exposure time [64]. The inset photos in Fig. 6C show the color changes of CDs-24 with the UV exposure time from amber yellow (before exposure) to green-yellow (after exposure for 24h). Moreover, no visible aggregation or precipitation is observed in the aqueous solutions of CDs after several months of storage at 25°C. The time-resolved PL decay results shown in Figs. 6D and S10 reveal three exponential decays in the PL intensity, indicating CDs with average lifetime decay components of 2.8–2.9nm for emission at 530nm. These values are comparable to the values of previous CDs synthesized using various methods or precursors [13,65–67]. The pH-dependent FL intensity, stability, and PL intensity decay results suggest that the CDs synthesized in this study are suitable for optoelectronic, photocatalytic, and biological applications.

The biocompatibility of CDs is one of the most important properties to consider for their applications in cell labeling and imaging. Hence, the cytotoxicity of the CDs derived from kraft lignin was evaluated using a human breast cancer cell line (HeLa cells) by employing the WST-8 colorimetric assay method [46] and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . The as-synthesized CDs show a very low cytotoxicity; the cells remained viable at a concentration of 500μgmL−1 after 48h of incubation, indicating the suitability of CDs for biological applications [7,8,44,56,68,69]. As shown in Figs. 8 and S11, the images of in vitro cellular uptake studies of CDs-24 with the HeLa cells confirm that the CDs can be employed as an FL probe in biomedical applications with high fluorescent signals.

The yields of the synthesized CDs are in the range 21–23wt% with 100g of CNPs or 6–7wt% with kraft lignin as the starting material, which are higher than those of previous reports (0.12–6.06wt% [29,70,71]). The high yield makes the current method be suitable for large-scale production of CDs.

We developed fluorescent CDs from kraft lignin using the clCO2 antisolvent method followed by carbonization and chemical oxidation. The as-synthesized CDs were well-dispersed, quasi-spherical, and nanometer-sized structures with an average diameter of 2.4nm and a very narrow size distribution. The structures were composed of a nanocrystalline carbon core with hydrophilic function groups (i.e., OH, COC, and COOH) on the surface, which are responsible for their excellent solubility in water and dispersibility in ethanol. The CDs showed broad absorption spectra in the UV–vis range with a sharp peak at 265nm and red shift wavelength, and they exhibited excitation-wavelength-independent (when excited from 280nm to 460nm) and excitation-wavelength-dependent (when excited from 480nm to 640nm) emission characteristics, which can be attributed to the effects of different emissive defect sites distributed on the surface of the CDs through chemical oxidation. The FL emission of the CDs ranged the entire visible wavelength region and extended into the near-infrared region with a maximum FL emission at 560nm when excited at 500nm. The trends of absorption and maximum FL emission intensities of CDs were stronger with longer reaction times, indicating a high degree of oxidation on the surface of the CDs and consequently, a high QY of CDs (13%) was achieved. The CDs exhibited pH-dependent FL emission behavior, excellent stabilities in a concentrated NaCl solution, and excellent photostability. Moreover, the CDs had very low cytotoxicity in high concentration with good imaging property in HeLa cells. This study demonstrated that the CDs derived from lignin using a simple and cost-effective method can be used as promising FL probes in bioimaging and biosensing with environmentally friendly and eco-friendly aspects.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIP) (grant numbers: 2018R1D1A1B07047129 and NRF-2016R1A2B3008800).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
